segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man, identifiable by his beard, glasses, and visible tattoos, wearing a light green striped shirt, standing inside a moving train. The rhythmic rumbling and clanking of the train are distinctly audible as he looks out at a passing industrial landscape, whispering, "Maybe this was a mistake," setting a contemplative tone. The view then shifts to a forward perspective from the train, showing tracks stretching into a desert under a bright blue sky, accompanied by the sustained sounds of the moving train. The man subsequently appears outside at a train station, featuring a covered platform with wooden beams and potted purple flowers, where he speaks directly to the camera. He explains that the United States has a love-hate relationship with train travel, historically boasting the biggest and most well-funded rail network, but now "that's not exactly the case anymore," as the US has "fallen woefully behind the rest of the world." He questions "what the heck happened?" and if the US can catch up, as on-screen graphics display a CBS News headline about Amtrak suspending service due to storm damage and a map showing a route from Los Angeles to New York City. The man reveals his plan to take the Amtrak from Los Angeles to New York City, a "70-hour journey," to find answers, holding up a small white object, possibly a ticket. He walks along the platform, continuing to speak, before a title card reading 'Vox Design, explained' appears. The segment concludes with the man entering an ornate building labeled 'Los Angeles,' presumably the starting point of his extensive train journey, as upbeat, jazzy background music begins.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with upbeat, slightly jazzy background music as a man with a beard, glasses, and colorful tattoos, wearing a light green striped short-sleeve shirt, stands on a Los Angeles train station platform, speaking directly to the camera. The scene transitions to the interior of a moving train, where the man continues his narration, explaining that his entire journey from Los Angeles to New York is "about four days long" and describing his current "living space" for "the next two days," humorously noting it "should be really good for my back" as someone who's "years old." As a map of the United States appears, illustrating his route from Los Angeles to New York City, he explains that this journey is "pretty different than it would have been about 100 years ago." The video then cuts to a close-up of a tray table displaying a U.S. rail system map with text overlay stating '1916, 254,000 MILES FREIGHT AND PASSENGER RAIL,' as he highlights that 1916 saw "the highest number there's ever been in this country" for railroad miles. He then holds up a red sign with the Pennsylvania Railroad logo, labeled 'Pennsy,' discussing "The Pennsylvania Railroad" or "the Pennsy," and making a distinct click sound while joking about the nickname. Vintage illustrations of a steam locomotive and historical train passengers appear as he emphasizes that it was "the largest corporation in the world at the time" and how "influential these companies were." The segment concludes with the man speaking again, now in a different, more modern train car, as the video transitions to a title card reading 'Vox Design, explained,' with the background music continuing throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a nostalgic black-and-white photograph of 19th-century passengers at a train station, as the man with a beard and tattoos narrates how trains were "interwoven into the fabric of American life," responsible for the "temples of their time" like Penn and Grand Central Stations. A hand then holds a sepia-toned 1911 photograph of Penn Station's grand, cathedral-like interior, showcasing its towering columns and ornate arches. The scene shifts to the modern train interior where the man, wearing glasses and a striped shirt, speaks directly to the camera, seated among blue seats with passing scenery visible outside, emphasizing how "amazing train travel could be back then." An overlaid vintage sketch of a luxurious train dining car with elegantly dressed passengers appears as he describes upscale trains featuring "chandeliers, barber shops, pianos, and food service that would make the Four Seasons look like Boston Market." This is followed by a historical Pennsylvania Railroad menu from November 13, 1917, highlighting a meat-free Tuesday and listing items like oysters, consommé, and "celery-fed duck," which he contrasts with his own experience, noting his train "didn't have ducks that were fed anything." A modern Amtrak flexible dining menu then appears, showing contemporary options like chicken ala rosa and baked ziti, before the man is seen eating "Thai Red Curry Street Noodles" in the train’s dining car, with a server attending other passengers, remarking that despite being a "microwave frozen package," they were "pretty good." The video transitions to the interior of a modern observation car with large windows and curved ceilings, where passengers relax and look out at the landscape, as the man concludes that while accommodations were "a far cry from crystal chandeliers," they were "comfortable enough." A distinct train horn sounds, followed by a conductor's announcement over the intercom, "Good evening, ladies and gentlemen, this is the conductor speaking. It has now been 5 hours and 15 minutes, which is the time it takes to fly from LAX..." as an animated map of Southern California highlights the train’s route from Los Angeles through San Bernardino and into the desert, marked with a yellow triangle.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an animated route map of Southern California, showing a red dotted line tracing a train’s path from Los Angeles into the desert, accompanied by a cheerful, almost whimsical background tune. A man's voice states they are en route to "Newark Airport" but are "still in California." The scene transitions to a dark, nighttime view from a moving train, with distant city lights, before cutting to a close-up of the man, with a beard, glasses, and colorful tattoos, wearing a light green striped shirt, vlogging inside a dimly lit train car. He explains it's "only 9 o'clock" but already dark, so there's "nothing to look out through the window," and "no Wi-Fi apparently," prompting him to "go to sleep." He notes the "overhead lights on all night" but is prepared with his "trusty eye mask" and "earplugs," anticipating their need due to "iPad kids who are watching movies at full volume with no headphones" nearby. He bids farewell, saying, "I'll see you in the morning." The camera pans to show the train’s interior with blue seats and a corridor, then cuts to the man brushing his teeth in a narrow train bathroom, followed by him smiling at the camera. A melancholic, accordion-like tune begins as he is shown sleeping in a reclined seat with headphones and a sleep mask, his legs propped up, with a '3:00 AM' timestamp appearing. His voice then narrates, questioning, "Did I sleep well last night? Hmm. I'm going to say no," elaborating that he was "literally kicked in the head by the woman in the seat next to you." He recounts "retreating to the observation car to sleep," which was "less than ideal." The video concludes with him waking up and eating a sandwich in the dining car, with daylight now visible through the window, indicating the passage of time during the journey.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man with a beard, glasses, and colorful tattoos, wearing a light green striped shirt, sipping coffee inside a modern train car. He speaks directly to the camera in a clear, articulate voice, explaining, "Like an astronaut in space, I'm trying to move around as much as I can so that my muscles don't deteriorate during my journey." He then walks through the train's corridor, passing a hand sanitizer dispenser and a 'CAUTION! WATCH YOUR STEP' sign, before entering a luxurious observation car. Seated in a standard blue seat, he continues speaking, "So, let's talk about how American trains went from gilded pleasure palaces to this." As the train moves through changing landscapes from greenery to desert, he elaborates, "Part of the reason why railroads became so wildly successful in the first place is because they were run by what were affectionately termed robber barons." Historical black-and-white portraits of 'Cornelius Vanderbilt' and 'Jay Gould' appear, identified by the speaker as "Men like Cornelius Vanderbilt, Jay Gould, James Fisk, and JP Morgan." He describes them as "ruthless, anti-labor, and really corrupt," as a vintage U.S. rail map zooms across the Midwest and South, and a historical illustration of a crowded train station with a 'SQUARE MEALS, LODGINGS' sign is shown. The man continues, "As a result, the train companies became pretty universally hated by the general public. As seen in what passed for scathing satire at the time." This leads to a political cartoon depicting Vanderbilt and Gould on trains, where a voiceover dramatically exclaims, "Now then, Jim, no jockeying, you know. Let him rip, Commodore. Don't stop to water, or you'll be beat!" The segment concludes with the man back in the modern train, speaking to the camera as the landscape outside shifts from green to arid, all set to a gentle, folksy background melody.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a man, bearded and wearing glasses and a dark blue collared shirt, seated inside a modern, moving train car, speaking directly to the camera. His voice is clear and conversational as he explains how the federal government reacted to railroad practices, instituting regulations like fixed rates and restrictions on fare changes, all while the subtle hum and rumble of the moving train are audible in the background. As he details these regulations, the screen cuts to a historical document titled 'Interstate Commerce Act of 1887,' displayed with a grid overlay and highlighted text. Returning to the man, he then quotes historian Robert Selph Henry, whose words appear on screen: 'The problem, to some minds at least, was how to keep railroads from making too much money.' He elaborates that these regulations initially weren't a major issue because railroads were still the most efficient way to travel, a point visually reinforced by archival black-and-white footage of a train moving through a wooded area. The narrative shifts as he describes how the advent of 'affordable automobiles' and 'air travel' changed everything, coinciding with archival footage of early 20th-century cars parked outside a brick building. A dynamic pie chart titled 'Passenger Travel in the US' then illustrates his point, showing that by 1956, only 4% of passenger travel was by train, a stark contrast to 98% in 1916. The man concludes his explanation back in the train, emphasizing that while train companies were no longer a monopoly, they were still regulated as if they were, as the scenery outside the window transitions from green to a darker, possibly evening light.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man with a beard, glasses, and a dark blue collared shirt, seated inside a modern train car, speaking directly to the camera. He animatedly explains that they are currently "being held on the tracks to let a freight train pass," setting the stage for a historical discussion. As a light, slightly melancholic instrumental background music begins, he elaborates on how "private commercial passenger trains have never been very profitable." The scene then cuts to an exterior shot of a freight train carrying colorful shipping containers moving across a concrete overpass, accompanied by a distinct train horn, as the man's voice continues, identifying "freight trains" as the "lifeblood of the industry." Returning to the man in the train interior, he explains how the government created "one company" to handle passenger service when rail companies struggled. This is illustrated by black-and-white archival footage of a steam locomotive moving through a wooded area, evoking early 20th-century rail travel. He reveals this company was "Amtrak," which stands for "the American Train Track." Following his rhetorical question about nicknames, an old-timey voiceover from an Amtrak commercial promotes its reach to "more than 450 cities in the USA," while a blue Amtrak logo graphic radiates lines to major U.S. cities. The man then reappears, concluding that "Amtrak was designed to fail from the beginning," as the train continues its journey through changing landscapes.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man, sporting a beard and glasses, in a dark blue collared shirt, seated inside a modern, moving train car. He speaks directly to the camera, animatedly explaining historical context, beginning with, "That may sound ridiculous, but let me explain." He details how the government created Amtrak as a "for-profit corporation," despite passenger service rarely being profitable, as the government "broke off all the passenger trains from the rail companies because they weren't making money." This narration is accompanied by a close-up of 'PUBLIC LAW 91-518—OCT. 30, 1970,' specifically 'TITLE III—CREATION OF A RAIL PASSENGER CORPORATION,' with 'for profit corporation' highlighted. The man continues, explaining Amtrak was tasked with "employing innovative operating and marketing concepts to develop the potential of modern rail service," as the document reappears, highlighting 'innovative operating and marketing concepts' and 'the establishment of improved reservations systems and advertising.' He then asks about initial funding, revealing, "Just $40 million." He notes, "Their budget has gone up a lot since then," stating, "Their budget for the year 2022 was $2.3 billion," which he acknowledges "sounds like a lot." An 'AMTRAK $2.3 billion' graphic appears, followed by a bar chart comparing 'FEDERAL HIGHWAYS' at $64.3 billion and 'AMTRAK' at $2.3 billion. The man emphasizes, "But when you take into account that the federal highway budget for the same year was $64.3 billion, you begin to see the inequity," continuing his explanation with expressive gestures as the train moves through changing landscapes, with a subtle train hum audible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man with a beard and tattoos, wearing a dark blue collared shirt and glasses, seated inside a modern train car. He speaks directly to the camera, stating that Amtrak's entire history's funding is less than one year's funding for the Interstate Highway system. This segues to a graphic comparing the US Highway budget ($64.3 billion) to Amtrak’s budget ($2.3 billion), with Amtrak's smaller allocation highlighted, as the man's voice continues, explaining this disparity shows America's travel priorities and why Amtrak has failed to expand or innovate. Gentle, melancholic background music with piano and light percussion begins as the scene transitions to an exterior shot of a train moving through Chicago, with the city skyline visible and a yellow triangle marker. The man then appears on a train station platform with passengers boarding, speaking to the camera, announcing his arrival in Chicago after 45 hours from Los Angeles. He then walks through a grand train station hall with high ceilings and columns, now in a light-colored shirt, humorously admitting he doesn't smell very good and mentioning he has a seven-hour layover in Chicago's Union Station, which he finds 'kind of amazing.' The background music continues as the scene shifts to an outdoor urban setting with tall buildings and a digital billboard, then to a wide-angle shot of the Cloud Gate sculpture (The Bean) in Chicago’s Millennium Park. The man reappears in front of the billboard, now in a black t-shirt, stating he got a chance to put on clean clothes, which 'feels amazing.' He continues, explaining he's in Millennium Park, 'rolling around with my suitcase like a dang tourist,' and trying to enjoy 'not being on a train as much as possible' before his next journey.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man, bearded and wearing glasses and a dark blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a bright, sunlit urban setting in front of a large digital billboard, announcing the "second and final leg of my journey" over upbeat, slightly jazzy background music. The scene transitions to a wide shot of Chicago’s Cloud Gate sculpture, reflecting the city skyline and bustling with people, before showing the man inside a train, unpacking his luggage in a sleeper car with blue seats. He then speaks to the camera, revealing he has "achieved the holy grail of my own private room" and demonstrates his "favorite feature": a compact, folding "hidden sink" in the train’s bathroom, accompanied by mechanical sounds, as he brushes his teeth. As train rumbling and clanking sounds mix with the music, he explains that this "dynamic" train's movements will "help rock me to sleep." The visuals shift to a nighttime industrial view from the train window, then to green hills in daylight, overlaid with a map graphic showing his train route from Chicago to Los Angeles. The music changes to a folksy tune as the man is seen lying in his sleeper berth, then sitting up, stating that upon waking, they had "reached Ohio," marking "2600 miles through my journey and 50-something hours." The segment concludes with the man discussing the "lot of reasons why people still want to take the train," including "the environment," as a title card from the Kleinman Center for Energy Policy appears, detailing his 3,000-mile Amtrak journey.

This transitions to Vox graphics reinforcing these statistics. The scene then shifts to a man with a beard, glasses, and a dark blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera from inside a modern train car. His calm, articulate voice explains that while flying and driving can be faster or cheaper, they are often "miserable experiences" due to TSA, flight delays, or being stuck in traffic. As passengers are seen boarding a train and a car drives in rainy weather, the man, now wearing headphones and a light-colored shirt, looks out a train window, continuing to speak about the freedom of train travel. He describes being able to "stare out the window and see the country go by," read, nap, or "wander around the train" and "stretch my legs." He is then seen relaxing in a sleeper car, wearing a black t-shirt with a city skyline graphic, emphasizing the enjoyment of having his "own private little room." A bar chart comparing the US Highway budget ($64.3 billion) to Amtrak’s budget ($2.3 billion) is displayed, visually supporting his point that Amtrak is "perpetually and profoundly underfunded." The man concludes by asking viewers to "imagine what it would be like if this were evened out," as a split-screen shows a hand with a phone displaying a Northeastern US rail route and the man walking through a train station platform. The segment ends with the man entering the grand Moynihan Train Hall in New York City, accompanied by a melancholy, orchestral melody featuring a prominent wind instrument.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, jazzy tune featuring saxophone and percussion, accompanying a wide-angle fisheye shot of a bustling New York City street with pedestrians and traffic. A man with a beard, glasses, and a dark blue collared shirt is then seen pulling a bright red suitcase, its wheels audibly rolling, past a double-decker bus. He enters a dimly lit hotel room, dropping the suitcase with a distinct thud before dramatically leaping onto the bed. Standing up, he turns to the camera, speaking directly to the viewer in a calm, reflective, and slightly enthusiastic voice, explaining, "So, I made it. All total it took me about 72 hours and 17 minutes." As he mentions the distance, on-screen text appears, reading '3,224 MILES,' which he notes he'll add in post. He continues, expressing that he "had a good time" and found the journey "actually really fun," describing it as a "really pleasant, memorable experience" where he "got to see parts of the country that I had never seen before." The scene transitions to him inside a moving train, now in a striped shirt, holding a camera with his tattooed arms visible, photographing the passing scenery. Black-and-white footage of a fence, trees, and tracks blurs past, followed by a color shot of the train crossing a bridge over a river amidst lush greenery, and finally, tracks rushing by from a moving perspective, all while he concludes that "it was a pleasant experience." The jazzy music continues as a new voice begins to speak about Amtrak.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a blurred, fast-moving shot of train tracks, accompanied by a male narrator's voice discussing the "1,000 miles of passenger rail in the US." This transitions to a clear, static view of a detailed Amtrak network map of the United States displayed on a tray table, highlighting various routes in red and green lines, as the narrator points out its "sparse coverage," "routes that don't connect," and the absence of trains in "Wyoming and South Dakota." He continues, suggesting that if the United States took Amtrak "off life support," it would "revolutionize the way people travel around the US." An exterior shot of a silver Amtrak train with red and blue stripes and the Amtrak logo is then shown, followed by a brick train station building with a large blue Amtrak sign and a modern glass facade. The scene cuts to the same bearded man, now identified as the narrator, in a dimly lit hotel room, speaking directly to the camera. He introduces a quote from former Amtrak President Graham Claytor, which then appears as a text graphic: "When I came to Amtrak I was convinced I could save it, but finally came to the conclusion that only the American public can save it when they say they want it." The narrator's voice reads this quote aloud. The man reappears in the hotel room, continuing his narration, stating he needs to "take a much-needed shower and go to sleep in a real bed" to "rest up for my flight back to California tomorrow." The video then transitions to the man seated inside a moving train, reclined in a blue seat with his eyes closed, appearing to rest. His voice, now sounding more relaxed and accompanied by the rhythmic sounds of the moving train, describes this experience as "one of the best parts of riding the Amtrak," enjoying "throwing on some PB Bridgers and wistfully staring out the window for hours on end." On-screen credits roll on the left side, listing production roles and sources including Amtrak and various music tracks like 'Big and Whimsical' and 'Cowboys All Ride Away'. The final shot shows a view from the train window, with trees and clouds passing rapidly, indicating the train’s speed, as the credits continue to roll. Throughout the segment, a gentle, whimsical background music plays.
